turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer has our opinion this morning in case 16-6855, Wilson v. Sellers.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Think of a state prisoner who has filed a petition in a federal habeas court and says give me writ of habeas corpus because I am being held unlawfully under federal law? Now, the habeas statute say that, normally, in order to show that he is being held unlawfully under federal law, he has to show that the state adjudication that says you should be held in person was contrary to or involved in unreasonable application of clearly established federal law or was based upon an unreasonable determination of the facts. Now, of course in order to decide this, the federal judge has to know what the state adjudication held, indeed what the last state court to have decided the matter held. Well, that is fairly easy where the last state court writes an opinion. But suppose the last state court to have heard this matter, the highest state court just says affirmed, or just says review denied, then what is the federal judge supposed to do? That is the question in this case. What's the answer? We hold that the federal habeas judge should then look through that single word opinion, look through it to the opinion of the highest court that did provide a relevant rationale. I mean, if the next highest court just said affirm, then look one further and keep going. But it should then presume after it finds that opinion, presume that the higher state court adopted the same reasoning. Now, we emphasize that this is only a presumption because the state might provide good reasons for believing that the higher state court in fact rested its holding even though it just said affirmed on a different rationale, but we still think that use of the presumption will prove an efficient way accurately to identify the rationale that did underlie the highest state court's one or two-word disposition of the matter. We elaborate our reasons further in our opinion. They are primarily resting on precedent, but because the Eleventh Circuit didn't imply this look-through approach, we reverse its decision and we remand the case for further proceedings. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justices Thomas and Alito have joined.
